outline.key_point: Think endometriosis nursing is a recent development? Think again—here’s how the role has evolved across the UK.
Historically, endometriosis patients in the UK had little specialist nursing support, leading to poor access and care.
The first endometriosis nurses appeared around 24 years ago—there were only about three across the UK.
In Wales, one nurse worked alone for years, highlighting regional disparities in support.
England saw endometriosis nurses embedded in specialist gynaecology teams, with growth accelerating after 2012.
The British Society of Gynaecology and Endoscopy’s 2012 criteria for endometriosis centres required a dedicated nurse, boosting the profession’s profile.
These developments have improved patient access, care quality, and support for those with endometriosis.
Understanding this journey can inspire the next generation of nurses to pioneer further advances in women’s health.

slides.subtext: Fact: It deserves attention and compassionate care—not dismissal.

slides.main_text: Empathetic healthcare makes a difference.
slides.subtext: Breaking the silence supports better sexual wellbeing for those with endometriosis.

slides.main_text: If you’re struggling, you’re not alone.
slides.subtext: Speak to your GP or a specialist—support and solutions are available.

carousel_draft.cta: Help break the silence—share this carousel to support endometriosis awareness and empathy.
carousel_draft.disclaimer: This content is for informational purposes only and does not replace medical advice. If you have health concerns, consult a qualified healthcare professional.

candidates.angle: Increased awareness and research funding improving endometriosis care
candidates.core_claim: Political advocacy and research advancements have raised awareness and improved understanding of endometriosis, though service provision still lags.
candidates.audience_pain: Slow service improvements despite increased awareness.
candidates.promise: Candidates can be part of a growing movement improving care.

source_citations.excerpt: I think people are more aware, it's definitely more political, particularly since your man in 2020 did the APGG, the All Party Parliamentary Group, that was brilliant, that was a real, you know, that put it right up there and has kept it up there.

We've now got three BSG centres in Wales, which is good. So people don't have to necessarily come all the way to Cardiff to have their surgery. So that's good. So there are good things happening, but it's not enough and it's not quick enough because girls are still suffering and still diagnosing women in their forties who've had problems since they were 12.
source_citations.note: Describes service improvements and ongoing challenges.

candidates.angle: Stress and resource limitations in endometriosis nursing roles
candidates.core_claim: Endometriosis nursing is a highly demanding and stressful role with insufficient resources and support.
candidates.audience_pain: High stress and burnout risk due to workload and lack of support.
candidates.promise: Awareness of challenges prepares candidates for the realities of the role and highlights the need for systemic improvements.
candidates.format_suggestion: Testimonial quotes carousel or day-in-the-life video snippets

candidates.topic: Advice for Newly Diagnosed or Suspected Endometriosis Patients
candidates.angle: Encouragement to persist and seek help
candidates.core_claim: Patients should work out their symptoms, use available resources, and keep seeking help without despair.
candidates.audience_pain: Feelings of hopelessness and barriers to diagnosis.
candidates.promise: Candidates can support and empower patients to advocate for themselves.

source_citations.excerpt: Work out your symptoms. Go to one of the, Endo UK is fantastic, Endometriosis Awareness. There's some really good resources. Go and have a look, but work out your symptoms. Look at yourself and say, oh, okay, what's happening? What's happening to me? And then go to somebody and say, this is what's happening to me. Can you help? But just keep knocking on the doors, you know. Don't despair, there are a lot of us out there who want to help.

candidates.audience_pain: Uncertainty about career progression and how to enter this niche field.
candidates.promise: Candidates can leverage existing clinical skills and leadership to specialise in endometriosis nursing.

candidates.core_claim: Longer appointment times, such as hour-long sessions, allow for better patient communication and care.
candidates.audience_pain: Short clinic times limit patient understanding and support.
candidates.promise: Candidates can advocate for and implement longer consultations to improve patient outcomes.

candidates.topic: Access to Endometriosis Nurses Across UK
candidates.angle: Variability in availability and referral pathways
candidates.core_claim: Access to endometriosis nurses varies across the UK, with some areas allowing self-referral and others relying on GP referrals, but overall services are limited and under-resourced.
candidates.audience_pain: Difficulty accessing specialised nursing support depending on location.
candidates.promise: Candidates can understand regional differences and advocate for improved access.
candidates.format_suggestion: Map-based carousel showing UK access points

candidates.angle: Importance of thorough assessment and specialist imaging
candidates.core_claim: Accurate diagnosis requires 360-degree laparoscopic assessment and specialist imaging like transvaginal scans and MRI interpreted by knowledgeable radiologists.
candidates.audience_pain: Misdiagnosis or incomplete diagnosis due to inadequate assessment.
candidates.promise: Candidates can learn the importance of detailed diagnostics to improve patient outcomes.

source_citations.excerpt: So I used to go to clinic. I used to do clinic where we'd have the consultants and me and then they'd see any of us. So people would come in and if they were new patients, I would take their history, do vaginal examination and do a transvaginal scan. So this is an internal scan with a long probe.

There are many diagnostic laparoscopies which are you need to do what we call a 360 degree assessment and I think everyone should ask for this. I tell every patient, whoever you're going to have your surgery with, say I want to have a 360 degree assessment, I want to have pictures to show that you've looked at my diaphragm, and that answers one of your questions.

candidates.angle: Excision preferred over ablation for deep endometriosis
candidates.core_claim: Excision surgery is more effective than ablation for treating deep endometriosis as ablation only removes surface lesions.
candidates.audience_pain: Patients receiving inadequate surgical treatment leading to persistent symptoms.
candidates.promise: Candidates can advocate for best surgical practices to improve patient outcomes.
candidates.format_suggestion: Before and after surgery explainer carousel

source_citations.excerpt: And then excision, not ablation. Because it's ablation only takes the surface. It's like skimming. It's like skimming something. If you need, if your plaster's gone on the wall, skimming it is not going to make any difference, it? Because it's still going to blow. Yeah? Same difference. Endometriosis is too deep to skim over the top of it. It goes too deep.

candidates.angle: Need for diverse specialists beyond nurses
candidates.core_claim: Effective endometriosis care requires a team including pelvic nurses, dieticians, psychologists, psychosexual counsellors, and consultants.
candidates.audience_pain: Limited access to comprehensive care teams.
candidates.promise: Candidates can appreciate the value of multidisciplinary collaboration.

source_citations.excerpt: But it's not just endonurses you need, you need pelvic nurses, you need dieticians, you need psychologists, you need psychosexual counselling, you need consultants.

candidates.angle: Availability of private clinics with multidisciplinary teams
candidates.core_claim: Private endometriosis centres, such as the one in Spire Hospital Cardiff, offer multidisciplinary care including surgery, psychology, and pain specialists.
candidates.audience_pain: Limited NHS access and long waits for surgery and specialist care.
candidates.promise: Candidates can explore private sector opportunities and patient pathways.

candidates.audience_pain: Patients may receive less accurate diagnostics if only abdominal scans are used.
candidates.promise: Candidates can learn to advocate for best diagnostic practices.

candidates.angle: Using symptom tools to communicate effectively with healthcare providers
candidates.core_claim: Keeping a detailed symptom diary helps patients communicate their condition clearly and aids diagnosis.
candidates.audience_pain: Short GP appointments and communication difficulties hinder diagnosis.
candidates.promise: Candidates can educate patients on symptom tracking to improve clinical conversations.

candidates.angle: New drugs and options like Dienogest offer alternatives to hormone therapies
candidates.core_claim: Dienogest is a newer treatment option that may reduce ovarian endometrioma size and is a preferable alternative to hormone therapies inducing menopause.
candidates.audience_pain: Side effects and limitations of traditional hormone treatments.
candidates.promise: Candidates can stay informed about evolving treatment options.

candidates.angle: Pelvic physio as a recommended but under-resourced treatment
candidates.core_claim: Pelvic physiotherapy is beneficial for endometriosis patients but is not widely available due to resource constraints.
candidates.audience_pain: Limited access to recommended physiotherapy services.
candidates.promise: Candidates can advocate for and integrate pelvic physio into care plans.

source_citations.excerpt: Pelvic physiotherapy, fantastic. Now the nice guidelines has always said that we should be using pelvic physio, but the resources have not been there, but people are becoming interested now.
source_citations.note: Describes guideline recommendations and resource issues.

candidates.angle: Continuous education and networking for endometriosis nurses
candidates.core_claim: Endometriosis nurses across the UK engage in regular education and are members of professional societies to improve care.
candidates.audience_pain: Need for ongoing training and professional support.
candidates.promise: Candidates can access structured learning and community support.

source_citations.excerpt: endometriosis nurses throughout the UK are all members of the BSG and they all attend regular education things. So everybody's being developed, but we just need to get there.
source_citations.note: Mentions professional society membership and education.

candidates.angle: Nurses performing scans to aid diagnosis and care
candidates.core_claim: Some endometriosis nurses are trained as sonographers, enabling them to perform vaginal and uterine scans to support diagnosis and treatment planning.
candidates.audience_pain: Limited access to specialist scanning in some settings.
candidates.promise: Candidates can expand their skills to include sonography.

source_citations.excerpt: I'm also a nurse sonographer, which means that I can scan the uterus. It's always very handy for endometriosis.

candidates.angle: Limited specialist services and emerging care pathways
candidates.core_claim: Thoracic endometriosis is rare with limited specialist services in Wales; nearby centres like Bristol are developing expertise.
candidates.audience_pain: Scarce specialist care for rare endometriosis manifestations.
candidates.promise: Candidates can be aware of rare conditions and referral pathways.

source_citations.excerpt: And your other question was, do we have specialists in Wales for thoracic endo? Well, we have, my answer to that would be no. However, we have done a few thoracic procedures. Bristol is developing thoracic more than us, and that's quite close to Wales, Bristol.

candidates.angle: Fatigue as a prevalent symptom of endometriosis
candidates.core_claim: Fatigue is a very common and often overlooked symptom experienced by up to 95% of endometriosis patients.
candidates.audience_pain: Symptoms like fatigue are dismissed or not linked to endometriosis.
candidates.promise: Candidates can learn to recognise and validate a wider range of symptoms.

candidates.topic: Need for Primary Care Gynaecology Specialists
candidates.angle: Advocating for primary care nurses specialising in gynaecology
candidates.core_claim: Primary care nurses specialising in gynaecology are essential to improve early diagnosis and management of endometriosis and other female health conditions.
candidates.audience_pain: Lack of accessible specialised care in primary settings leads to delays and poor management.
candidates.promise: Candidates can see opportunities to pioneer and advocate for primary care roles.

source_citations.excerpt: My ultimate goal, and I present yearly or twice a year, biannually in the primary care nursing, which is in England, predominantly conferences. And my aim, the reason I go there is what I want is I want a primary care nurse who, like a diabetic nurse, like an asthma nurse, is based in primary care and therefore totally accessible to people close to home.
source_citations.note: Describes vision for primary care nursing.

source_citations.excerpt: We need a solid base in primary care that looks at gynaecology, not necessarily endometriosis, but all things gynaecology, because most gynaecology conditions are resolvable.

candidates.angle: School-based education and public awareness campaigns
candidates.core_claim: Education in schools about periods and endometriosis symptoms is crucial for early recognition and support.
candidates.audience_pain: Lack of early awareness leads to delayed diagnosis and stigma.
candidates.promise: Candidates can participate in or support educational initiatives.

source_citations.excerpt: We've got Menstrual Project, again a Welsh group but this is open to everybody and they particularly have done fantastic work in schools. We've got Bloody Matters. which is a government-driven thing in Wales, and they've got a similar where we go into schools and we talk about periods.

It's getting that sort of that level of recognition for, know, Joe was sitting next to me and he notices that I keep having to to the toilet. And then points out to the teacher and the teacher goes, that's happening every month. I know what that might be.
source_citations.note: Shows impact of awareness in schools.
